local_names.romanized_local: Dāng Guī Kǎo Yā Bàn Zhī

aliases: Dāng Guī Kǎo Yā Bàn Zhī

romanized: Dāng Guī Kǎo Yā Bàn Zhī

food_subgroup: Poultry — roasted duck with herbal marinade

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for dang gui (angelica root) marinated roast duck. FOODON_03302051 (duck food product) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LK (Duck meat) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for herbal-marinated roast duck preparations in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for dang gui roast duck as a prepared dish.

main_ingredients: dang gui (Angelica sinensis / Chinese angelica root)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is half a roast duck, approximately 550 g, typically shared between two to three diners or consumed as a single large meal portion.

nutrient_source.title: Roasted Duck, Chinese-style — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 05141) and HPB Singapore Foodo analogous roast duck entry
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central roasted duck (skin on, NDB 05141 / FDC ID 171478) as the closest approved analogue for Chinese-style roast duck. Dang gui (angelica root) is used in small quantities as a herbal marinade and does not materially alter the macronutrient profile of the duck. Sodium is adjusted upward to reflect soy-sauce-based marinade typical of Chinese roast duck preparations, consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo guidance on Chinese roast duck dishes. Values represent the edible portion including skin.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Protein- and Fat-Dominant Foods — University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.source_match_notes: Roast duck is a high-fat, high-protein food with negligible carbohydrate content (0 g per 100 g). Pure protein and fat foods have a glycemic index of effectively 0 because they contain no glycemic carbohydrates. Per the Veritas v2 critical rule for zero-carbohydrate foods, `glycemic_index` is set to `1` (the lowest valid positive value) and `gi_category` is 'low'. This convention is applied because the food contains no glycemic carbohydrates and will not directly raise blood glucose. `glycemic_load_per_serving` = 0.0 because carbohydrate_g = 0. This is confirmed zero per the nature of the food: carbohydrate_g is naturally zero for plain roasted duck meat with herbal marinade containing no starch or sugar in meaningful quantity.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy-sauce-based marinade (~520 mg sodium per 100 g; ~2860 mg per half-duck serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid dang gui roast duck due to its very high sodium load from soy sauce marinade; a half-duck serving may provide over 100% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content but high saturated fat and caloric density
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dang gui roast duck contains virtually no carbohydrate and will not directly raise postprandial blood glucose; however, its high saturated fat content and caloric density may contribute to insulin resistance and weight gain if consumed frequently, so portion control is advised for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (28.4 g/100 g), saturated fat (7.6 g/100 g), cholesterol (89 mg/100 g), and sodium (520 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume dang gui roast duck sparingly, as the combination of high saturated fat, dietary cholesterol, and sodium poses compounded cardiovascular risk; a half-duck serving delivers approximately 156 g of fat and 490 mg of cholesterol.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Half Dang Gui Duck (当归烤鸭半只) is a Chinese herbal roast duck dish popular in Singapore, where the whole duck is marinated and roasted with dang gui (Angelica sinensis, Chinese angelica root), soy sauce, dark soy sauce, five-spice powder, garlic, ginger, Shaoxing rice wine, and sesame oil before being portioned into halves for sale. The dish originates from Chinese medicinal-culinary tradition, where dang gui is prized for its warm, slightly bitter, and aromatic flavour profile and its reputed blood-nourishing (补血) properties in Traditional Chinese Medicine. The roasted duck skin is lacquered, crispy, and deeply savoury, while the meat is tender, juicy, and infused with herbal fragrance. Nutritionally, a half-duck serving (~550 g) is calorie-dense (~1854 kcal), high in protein (~104 g) and total fat (~156 g), with negligible carbohydrate. The high sodium content (~2860 mg per serving) from the soy-sauce marinade is a significant concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for halal diets unless certified, and contains soy and gluten allergens. It is typically served as a main course, shared among two to three diners, accompanied by steamed rice or noodles. In Singapore, it is sold at Chinese roast-meat stalls (烧腊档) and specialty restaurants such as Hennessy Duck.
common_user_queries: How many calories in half a dang gui duck?
How much sodium in roast duck?
What does dang gui duck taste like?
Where to buy dang gui duck in Singapore?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central roasted duck (skin on) as the closest approved analogue, with sodium adjusted to reflect soy-sauce marinade. No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry for dang gui duck was found; the adaptation is well-reasoned and consistent with the food's composition, but direct measurement data for this specific dish is not available from an approved primary source.
notes: 1. carbohydrate_g is 0.0: naturally zero — roasted duck with herbal marinade contains no starch or sugar in nutritionally significant quantities; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central roasted duck analogue. sugar_g is 0.0: naturally zero for the duck meat and herbal marinade base; trace glazing sugar is below detection threshold in the per-100g context. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: naturally zero for a meat-based dish with no plant fibre components in the edible portion. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: naturally zero for roasted duck meat; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 2. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this food contains no glycemic carbohydrates, per Veritas v2 critical rule for zero-carbohydrate foods; gi_category is 'low' per convention. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g = 0. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entry for roasted duck. 4. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 and FoodEx2 A00LK are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for dang gui herbal roast duck. 5. Halal status is false as standard Chinese roast duck preparations use Shaoxing rice wine (alcohol) and are not certified halal unless specifically stated by the restaurant. 6. Serving size of 550 g represents the approximate edible yield of half a standard roast duck (skin and meat, bone excluded from weight). 7. Source restaurant: Hennessy Duck — Funan, Singapore (field survey data listing). 8. Price: SGD 35.00 per half duck.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built complete record from scratch. Adapted nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central FDC 171478 (roasted duck, skin on) with sodium adjustment for soy-sauce marinade. Added all mandatory multilingual names (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Documented all zero-value macros as naturally zero. Set GI = 0 with gi_category = low for zero-carbohydrate food. Used closest_parent ontology codes with gap notes. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases. All hard gates and Q1–Q8 criteria satisfied in first iteration.

Iteration 2: Corrected `glycemic_index` from 0 to 1 as per Veritas v2 critical rule for zero-carbohydrate foods, which requires GI to be a numeric value greater than zero. Updated `gi_source.source_match_notes` and main `notes` to reflect this change and justification. Updated `veritas_qc.q4_glycemic_completeness` status and notes.
